title: Reading your smart meter's in-home display
subtitle: What the IHD shows, how to interpret it, and how to use it to cut consumption
excerpt: The in-home display (IHD) that comes with a smart meter shows current electricity and gas consumption in real time. The consistent findings from smart meter trials: households that actively use the IHD to change behaviour save 3-6% on energy bills versus those who ignore it.
sourceNotes: BEIS smart meter customer experience study (2022). Energy Saving Trust smart meters and IHD guidance. Ofgem IHD standards requirements for smart meter installations.
aliases: how to use smart meter display

glossaryTerms.definition: A gas or electricity meter that sends half-hourly readings automatically to your supplier. A separate in-home display unit (IHD) shows real-time consumption and cost. The IHD is not the meter: it is a wireless display that communicates with the meter hub.

In-home display. The small wireless unit supplied with a smart meter installation that shows current electricity and gas usage in kWh, watts, or pence-per-hour. The IHD must be kept within range of the meter's communications hub (typically the electricity meter location).

The minimum electricity consumption of a home when all deliberate devices are off: standby loads from TVs, set-top boxes, routers, fridges, and freezers. Typical UK household baseload is 200-500 W. High baseload indicates devices drawing more than expected.

content.text: The
IHD

shows current consumption in two ways: instantaneous power draw in watts (or kilowatts) and estimated spend in pence per hour or pounds per day. The real-time wattage reading is the more useful: it lets you find out what individual appliances draw by turning them on and off and watching the reading change.
Finding your baseload
Turn off everything you can: all lights off, dishwasher off, washing machine off, kettle off. Leave the fridge and freezer running. Read the IHD. This is your
baseload

. Typical range: 200-500 W. A reading above 500 W with everything off suggests a high-draw standby device: satellite receiver, old set-top box, network switch with inefficient power supply, or an old fridge running inefficiently.
A 400 W baseload running continuously uses 3.5 kWh/day: approximately 1,300 kWh/year. Reducing it by half cuts that consumption accordingly. Finding the cause is worth the effort.
Identifying high-draw appliances
Stand at the IHD and switch appliances on one at a time. Note the increase in watts. Typical draws:
Kettle: 2,000-3,000 W. Brief but high.
Oven: 1,500-3,000 W during preheat, lower once stabilised.
Dishwasher: 1,000-2,000 W during the heating phase, 200-400 W during wash and drain.
Tumble dryer: 2,000-3,000 W continuously for a full cycle.
LED TV (50 inch): 80-120 W.
Halogen spotlights (12 x 50 W): 600 W. Replacing with LED (12 x 5 W) saves 540 W whenever the lights are on.
Daily and weekly totals

Most IHDs show a daily kWh total for electricity and (where gas is metered separately, which is usual) a daily gas kWh total. The gas total is the most variable from day to day and most responsive to heating behaviour. A heating-season baseline for a 3-bedroom semi in the Midlands is approximately 25-40 kWh/day of gas. On an unusually cold day (5°C outside), gas consumption rises to 40-60 kWh; on a mild day (12°C outside), it drops to 10-20 kWh. Comparing daily gas use with the outside temperature reveals how the house responds to weather and whether behaviour changes (turning the thermostat down by 1°C, reducing heating hours by 30 minutes) have the expected effect.
The IHD's limitations
The
smart meter

's IHD does not show which device is drawing power, only the total load. For more granular analysis, a plug-in energy monitor (plugged into a single socket) or a whole-house clamp meter that shows per-circuit breakdown is more useful. The IHD also does not typically show solar export or import breakdown if a solar system is installed; the solar inverter's own monitoring is the right tool for that.
